segments.s521-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is past tense 'came'. به نزدیکی بارگاه is a prepositional phrase ('to/into the vicinity of the palace') giving place; word order places the temporal conjunction before the clause and the location phrase after the verb.
segments.s521-g2: بگفتند is past plural 'they said/told'. با شاه here means 'in the presence of / to the king' (classical usage of با). زان is the contracted/poetic form 'from that / about that', and بارخواه is 'petitioner'; together they mean 'they told the king about that petitioner.'
segments.s521-g3: جوان را uses را as the definite object marker ('the young man' as object). به مهر اردوان is an ablative/instrumental sense 'by/with Ardavan's favour' or 'in Ardavan's kindness'. پیش خواند is a compound: پیش 'before / in the presence of' and خواند here is past 'called/summoned' — so 'he summoned him before Ardavan.'
segments.s521-g4: ز introduces the topic 'about' (poetic use). بابک is the name. سخنها (plural) 'words/talks' modified by فراوان 'many/much'. براند is a past/perfective verb from راندن used metaphorically 'drove forth' → 'uttered' or 'spoke' many words; here it functions as the main verb of the clause.
segments.s521-g5: به نزدیکی تخت is a locative phrase 'near the throne'. بنشاختش is an older/epic past transitive form meaning 'he set/placed/seat(ed) him' — the prefix ب- + past stem and the final -ش is the object suffix 'him'.
segments.s521-g6: به برزن 'in/to the courtyard/plain'. یکی جایگه is an indefinite 'a certain place' (یکی modifying جایگه). ساختش is past 'made/built' with the object suffix '-ش' ('he made/built it/for him'); together: 'he made a place for him in the courtyard.'
segments.s521-g7: فرستاد is past 'he sent/dispatched'. هرگونه‌ای modifies خوردنی: 'every/any kind of edible' — here خوردنی is a noun meaning 'food/edible items'. The adjective phrase follows the verb as the sent object.
segments.s521-g8: ز introduces items in a list 'of/including'. پوشیدنی is 'wearables/garments'. هم 'also'. the second ز again lists 'and also' گستردنی — 'things to be spread/coverings' (cloths, spreads); the whole is a compact poetic listing of provisions sent.
segments.s521-g97: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning “near/close to” (به + نزدیک). اسپان سرایی is a genitive/attributive phrase: اسپان “horses” with سرایی (noun from سرای/سراییدن) meaning “house/stable.” گزید is the simple past, 3rd-person singular (“he chose/selected”), placed at the end as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s521-g98: نه negates the following clause. اندر is the preposition “in/within.” خور کار functions as a compound describing the eating/feeding or dining area (خور = eating/meal, کار = work/affair), and جایی گزید is “he chose a place” with گزید again the past tense verb at the end; the whole clause means he did not take a place among/inside the dining/feeding place.
segments.s521-g99: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase “night and day.” خوردن is a verbal noun (gerund) “eating,” here functioning as the activity/predicate. بدی modifies کار (بدی کار = “evil/bad deed/work”), and اوی is the possessive “his,” so the phrase says that eating (and generally evil doing) was his occupation day and night; word order places the verbal noun and its modifiers before the final possessive and (implied) copula, typical of older Persian.
segments.s521-g100: می‌و جام is a conventional pair meaning wine and cup (می used figuratively as fermented fruit/wine). The nouns are linked by و, followed by رامشگران (“comforters/entertainers”) as another item in the list. یار اوی is a predicate nominal meaning “his companions” (یار = companion; اوی = his); the copular verb is omitted (a common feature), so the list is said to be his companions.
segments.s521-g33: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone' placed before the noun. بادپایان (plural) is a compound meaning 'the fleet‑footed' or 'swift runners.' برانگیختند is a past tense, 3rd‑person plural verb ('they sprang up/rose'), coming after its subject as is common in Persian poetry; the clause literally reads 'All the swift‑footed they‑sprang‑up.'
segments.s521-g34: همی is a poetic preverbal particle here (an emphatic/continuous/linking adverb, 'meanwhile/then/just') that introduces the next action. گرد functions as 'dust' (the dust kicked up by the runners) and is the noun subject; با + خوی is a prepositional phrase 'with their own/with their fellows' (خوی as 'kin/fellows'); برآمیختند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they mingled/blended.' Word order is typical poetic Persian: adverb – subject – prepositional phrase – verb.
segments.s521-g35: همی is the same poetic adverbial particle ('then/straightaway'). تاخت is a past tense verb 'galloped/rushed.' پیش here means 'before/in front of' or 'toward the presence of' (indicating direction toward someone), and اندرون is 'inner (quarters/area).' Proper name اردشیر follows, so the phrase reads 'he galloped before/into Ardashir's inner court.'
segments.s521-g36: چو + نزدیک + شد forms a temporal clause 'when he became/grew near' (چو = 'when'). شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/was' used here with نزدیک ('near/approaching'). در کمان راند تیر is a compact clause with object‑first poetic order: در کمان 'into/onto the bow,' راند is past 'he drove/put (in),' and تیر is the object 'arrow' — literally 'he drove an arrow into the bow.'
segments.s521-g37: بزد is the 3rd‑person past 'he struck/smote' and appears first (verb‑first is common in epic lines). بر + سرون is a prepositional phrase 'upon the cypresses' (سرون = tall cypresses/tall beauties). یکی گور نر is a noun phrase 'one male gūr' (here گور is the wild ass/onager and نر marks 'male'), the direct object. Word order is poetic: verb, adpositional phrase, then object.
segments.s521-g38: گذر کرد is a compound verbal expression 'made/pass(ed) over' (گذر 'passage' + کرد 'did/made' functioning together as a verb). بر introduces the target 'over/on' and گور here again refers to the onager (same word as above). پیکان و پر are the things that 'passed' — 'an arrow and a feather' — with و simply linking them. The clause thus says an arrow and a feather passed over/through the onager.
segments.s521-g39: بیامد is past 'came.' هم following the verb or verb phrase means 'also/too' (marks that this arrival is in addition to the others). اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the time (of),' and اردوان is the proper name 'Ardavan.' The literal order is 'came also in the time Ardavan,' i.e. 'Ardavan also came at that time.'
segments.s521-g40: بدید is the past 'he saw.' آن گشاد is a noun phrase where آن = 'that' and گشاد is used with its sense 'gaping/open' (so 'that which gaped/that opening'). The conjunction و links to بر آن جوان, where بر = 'upon/on' and آن جوان = 'that young man.' Poetic elliptic order omits repeated verbs: 'he saw that it gaped, and (he saw) that young man upon it.'
segments.s521-g73: دلش (del+ـش) is 'his heart' with the possessive enclitic ـش. گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', so دلش گشت = 'his heart became'. زان is the archaic fused form of از + آن 'from/ because of that', and کار here means 'affair/deed' (object of زان). پر before a noun phrase means 'full (of)', so پر درد و رنج = 'full of pain and sorrow' (درد و رنج joined by و). The clause reads literally 'his heart became, from that affair, full of pain and sorrow.'
segments.s521-g74: بیاورد is a past tense narrative form 'he brought/caused to bring' (archaic poetic past). دینار is the direct object 'dinar(s)' and چندی qualifies it as 'some/a few'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and گنج is 'treasure' or treasure-house; the phrase means 'he brought some dinars from the treasure.'
segments.s521-g75: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatched'. نزدیک او is an adverbial phrase 'near him' / 'to his vicinity' placed before the numeral; ده هزار is the numeral 'ten thousand' (object/amount). The word order places the destination/adverbial before the number of forces sent.
segments.s521-g76: هیونی (pl. of هیون) here names the force/creatures/war-units (literal 'beasts' or fierce creatures in the lexicon); برافگند is a past form meaning 'cast up/raised' or 'scattered/caused to fly up'. گرد in this context is 'dust' (the dust raised by movement) and سوار is 'riders/horsemen.' Grammatically the verb governs the following nouns: '(they) raised dust and (the) horsemen (appeared/rode),' i.e. a sudden appearance/scattering associated with the dispatched force.
segments.s521-g77: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that/that'), followed by پیش او شد دبیر — literally 'a scribe came before him' where شد functions as the past auxiliary/predicate 'became/came to be' and دبیر is the predicative noun 'scribe'. The clause means 'he ordered that a scribe be brought into his presence.'
segments.s521-g78: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an unspecified person or thing). نامه is the direct object 'a letter' and فرمود here continues the polite/narrative 'he had (it) composed/issued' (the verb is used narratively). زی اردشیر is an archaic prepositional phrase; زی is used here in the sense 'to/for' (classical/poetic usage) with اردشیر 'Ardashir' as its complement, so 'a letter was (sent) to/for Ardashir.'
segments.s521-g79: که is the relative/complementizer 'that' introducing the clause. این 'this' points to the person just mentioned. کم خرد is a compound meaning 'of little wisdom' or 'ill-advised' (کم qualifies خرد 'wisdom' to mean 'lacking judgment'). نورسیده is a past-participle adjective 'newly born' used idiomatically as 'inexperienced', and جوان is 'young (man)'. The sequence is noun phrases piled as attributive modifiers: 'this little-witted, inexperienced young man.'
segments.s521-g80: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رفتی is the past stem 'he went' (narrative past). به introduces the purpose/place 'to' or 'on' (here 'to the hunt' / 'hunting'), نخچیر means 'game/prey' or by extension 'a hunting expedition' and با اردوان gives the companion 'with Ardavan'. The clause reads 'when he went on a hunt with Ardavan.'
segments.s521-g17: بدید is a past (preterite) verb 'saw' placed before its nominal subject (اردوان) — a common classical/poetic order. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پسند آمدش is a psychological construction: پسند آمد literally 'it came as pleasing', with the enclitic -ش on آمد indicating the beneficiary/experiencer ('to him' / 'he'), so the whole clause means 'he liked him' or 'it pleased him'.
segments.s521-g18: جوانمرد را uses را as the definite/object marker to topicalize 'the young man'. سودمند is a predicate adjective 'useful' placed before آمدش; آمدش again is the 'آمد' + enclitic -ش meaning 'it came to him' — idiomatically 'he regarded (him) as useful' or 'he found the young man useful'. The order (adjective + آمد) is a poetic way to express 'seemed' / 'was found to be'.
segments.s521-g19: پسروار is an adverbial/qualifying word 'like a son' modifying the following clause. خسرو is the subject 'the king'. همی is the habitual/continuous particle (classical) meaning 'used to' / 'continually'. داشتش is the past verb 'had/kept him' with enclitic -ش marking the object 'him'. Together: 'the king kept him as if a son'.
segments.s521-g20: زمانی here functions temporally ('for a time' / 'even for a moment') and begins the adverbial phrase. به تیمار literally 'to/into care' (i.e. 'in care' / 'unattended'); نگذاشتش is the negative past 'did not leave him' (neg. of گذاشتن) with enclitic -ش as object pronoun — idiomatically 'he did not leave him without care' or 'he never let him be neglected'.
segments.s521-g21: به introduces a list of occasions/places ('at/for'): می‌خوردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'eating' (i.e. meals), خوان is 'banquet/table', and نخچیرگاه is 'hunting-ground'. The three items are enumerated as the settings where the man was kept/present.
segments.s521-g22: به پیش خودش is a prepositional phrase 'in his (own) presence' (پیش = 'in front of / in the presence of', خودش = 'himself / his own'). داشتی is a past-form of داشتن used here in epic/poetic narrative to mean 'he kept/held' (past habitual); سال و ماه 'years and months' gives the duration ('for years and months').
segments.s521-g23: همی is the habitual/continuous particle ('used to' / 'still'), داشتش is 'had/kept him' with enclitic -ش. همچو + فرزند خویش = 'like a child of his own' (همچو = 'like', فرزند = 'child', خویش = 'his own/kinship'), so the clause means 'he kept him as if he were his own son.'
segments.s521-g24: جدایی is the noun 'separation'. ندادش is the negative past 'did not give' with enclitic -ش (here idiomatically 'did not cause separation' — i.e. 'did not separate him'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. پیوند خویش 'his own bond/ties' (پیوند = bond, خویش = his own). Together: 'he did not separate him from his own ties.'
segments.s521-g57: بدان is an imperative built from the verbal root دان with the old prefix ب- (so read as 'know' or 'be aware'). تا here introduces the content clause ('that') rather than a temporal 'until'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'beyond', and بگذری is a 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive (classical Persian form) meaning 'you pass/exceed' — together: 'Know that you will pass/exceed my son.'
segments.s521-g58: This short coordinate phrase contains two nouns/verb-forms linked by و. بلندی is a noun meaning 'height/loftiness' (here used as the quality to be chosen). گزینی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive or jussive of گزینیدن 'to choose' (so: 'you should/you will choose'). کنداوری is a proper name (a person or place) presented as the second thing to be chosen.
segments.s521-g59: برو is the imperative 'go'. تازی اسْپان is a noun phrase 'Tazi-horses' (Tazi = Arabian/Arabian-style; اسپان is the plural of اسپ 'horses'). ما را uses ما as the possessor/topic 'our' with را marking the direct object (emphatic/definite object marker). ببین is the imperative 'see' or 'look at', so together: 'Go, look at our Tazi horses.'
segments.s521-g60: هم means 'also' or 'besides' here, آن جایگه is 'that place/spot' (آن = that, جایگه = place/position). بر is the preposition 'on/at', and سرایی is an infinitival/stative verbal noun meaning 'to dwell/house or to make a house' (here 'to settle or build a dwelling'). گزین is an imperative/infinitive form of 'choose' — 'also choose that spot as a place to dwell.'
segments.s521-g61: بران is an archaic/poetic location adverb meaning 'upon/thereon' (here roughly 'over them' or 'in that role'). آخر modifies and functions as 'last' or 'ultimate'. اسپ سالار combines اسپ 'horse' with سالار 'chief/commander' = 'commander of the horses'. باش is the imperative 'be' — 'Be the final commander of the horses.'
segments.s521-g62: به هر کار is a prepositional phrase 'in/for every affair' (به + هر + کار). با هر کسی is 'with every person' (با = with, هر کسی = any/everyone). یار باش is an imperative 'be a companion/ally' — overall: 'In every task, be an ally/companion to everyone.'
segments.s521-g63: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. پر از here is the adjective phrase 'full of' (پر + از). آب چشم literally 'water of the eye' = tears; this is a poetic way to say 'with eyes full of tears'. اردشیر is the subject (proper name). The whole segment reads: 'Ardashir came, his eyes full of (tears).'
segments.s521-g64: بر آخر اسپ is a locative or positional phrase 'onto/on the rear of the steed' (بر = on/upon, آخر = rear/end, اسپ = horse). شد ناگزیر is a compound meaning 'became compelled' or 'was forced' (شد = became; ناگزیر here functions as 'compelled' rather than abstract 'inevitable') — 'he was forced onto the back of the horse.'
segments.s521-g89: هرانگه که is a temporal conjunction ('whenever/when'); این مایه بردی بکار is a verb phrase in which بردی is the past second‑person form of بردن ('you took/you put'), and بکار (بِ + کار) acts as a verbal complement meaning 'into use / to use' — literally 'you took this fund to use.' Word order is subjectless (the subject is implicit) and the clause sets the time/condition for what follows.
segments.s521-g90: دگر خواه is an instruction-like clause: دگر 'another/different' modifies the verb خواه ('wish/want/choose'), here functioning as an exhortation 'choose something else' or 'act otherwise.' تا + بگذرد establishes the durational goal 'until' plus بگذرد (present/subjunctive form meaning 'it passes') and روزگار ('time'); together: 'until time passes.'
segments.s521-g91: This is a noun phrase in the typical Persian order: head noun تگاور ('rider/warrior') followed by modifiers. هیون is an epithet/attribute (literally 'beast' or 'of beasts') and جهاندیده پیر are adjectival modifiers 'world‑wise' and 'old' that follow the noun, giving a compound descriptive title.
segments.s521-g92: بیامد is the simple past 'he came.' دوان is a manner adverb/participle 'running/galloping' that follows the verb to show how he came. تا here marks the extent/direction 'as far as / up to' and with the following preposition بر ('to/toward') yields 'came galloping up to Ardashir.'
segments.s521-g93: چو ('when') introduces the temporal clause. آن نامه برخواند is a past action: برخواند is a compound verb meaning 'read aloud/recited,' and خرسند گشت uses the adjectival predicate خرسند ('pleased') with گشت ('became') to form 'he became pleased.' The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s521-g94: دلش is 'his heart' with the possessive suffix -ش. سوی is the directional postposition 'toward'; نیرنگ و اروند are the two things toward which his heart turns; گشت again is 'turned/became.' The clause uses repeated گشت for parallelism: 'his heart turned toward X and Arvand.'
segments.s521-g95: بگسترد is a past/optative form of گستردن with the prefix بـ, here 'he spread out' or 'he set out.' هرگونه is a quantifier 'every kind of,' and گستردنی is a deverbal adjective/noun 'that which can be spread' (i.e., 'spreadable items' or 'things to be laid out'), so the phrase means 'he spread out every kind of thing to be spread.'
segments.s521-g96: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from.' پوشیدنیها is the plural 'wearables/garments' (پوشیدنی + ها). و 'and' links the two categories, and از خوردنی uses از + خوردنی ('edible/food') to mean 'from food' — together: 'from garments and from edible things.'
segments.s521-g25: چنان بد که — an idiomatic conjunctional phrase meaning 'so (much) that' or 'so it was that'. چنان (so/thus) modifies بد (here adverbial: 'badly/so much'); که introduces the clause that follows. روزی functions as a temporal adverb 'one day' (classical Persian often uses روزی = 'one day'); به + نخچیرگاه marks the place ('to/at the hunting-ground'). Word order places the temporal and locative phrase before the main clause, as common in older Persian.
segments.s521-g26: پراگنده شد لشکر و پور شاه — predicate + subject(s). پراگنده شد is a compound verb meaning 'became scattered / were scattered' (پراگنده = scattered; شد = became/was), with passive/resultative sense. لشکر و پور شاه are the coordinated subjects/affected parties ('the army and the king's sons'); پور شاه = 'the king's sons' (پور = son + شاه = king). Classical Persian often puts verb before nominative elements.
segments.s521-g27: همی راند با اردوان اردشیر — همی is an iterative/progressive adverb 'continually / was continually'; راند is the past/punctual form 'drove / chased / caused to go'; با اردوان is an adjunct 'with Ardavan'. Here the verb appears before the subject (شاه ... ) which follows later — a typical classical word order where the action is stated and the subject can come after the object.
segments.s521-g28: جوانمرد را شاه بد دلپذیر — this places the object (جوانمرد را 'the valiant/ chivalrous one' marked by را) before the subject phrase (شاه بد دلپذیر). را is the definite/object marker. شاه بد دلپذیر is the subject phrase: شاه 'the king' modified by بد دلپذیر (بد + دلپذیر = 'not-pleasing / ill-pleasing' = 'unpleasant, ill-disposed'); بد here negates/qualifies دلپذیر. So the whole segment means 'the unpleasant king (subject) [drove] Ardashir the brave (object)'.
segments.s521-g29: پسر بود شاه اردوان را چهار — an inverted existential/possessive construction. The core is 'شاه اردوان را چهار پسر بود' meaning 'King Ardavan had four sons'; here پسر (son) + بود (was/there were) and the possessor 'شاه اردوان را' is placed before the numeral چهار (four). را marks the possessor phrase. Classical order often varies; translate as 'King Ardavan had four sons.'
segments.s521-g30: ازان هر یکی چون یکی شهریار — a distributive descriptive clause: ازان 'of those' + هر یکی 'each one' + چون یکی (an idiom 'one like another') + شهریار 'a ruler/king'. The structure says that each of those (sons) was a ruler, i.e. 'each one, like the others, was a king.'
segments.s521-g31: به هامون پدید آمد از دور گور — locative first: به هامون 'on/into the plain'; پدید آمد 'appeared / came into view' (پدید = 'visible/manifest', آمد = 'came'); از دور 'from afar' modifies the coming; گور is the thing that appeared. In the hunting context گور is best read as the animal 'gūr' (onager/wild ass), so 'from afar a gūr appeared on the plain.'
segments.s521-g32: ازان لشکر گشن برخاست شور — cause / result clause: ازان 'from that / from them' + لشکر گشن 'the hungry host/troops' (لشکر modified by گشن 'hungry') + برخاست 'rose up' (past) + شور 'tumult/commotion'. The phrase means 'from them the hungry troops rose up in tumult' or more naturally 'a tumult arose as the hungry troops rose.'
segments.s521-g81: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. تاختی is the 2nd-person singular past of تاختن ('you charged/rushed'); in classical Persian verbs often follow sentence adverbs. پیش فرزند اوی is a prepositional/goal phrase 'upon/in front of his son' where پیش = 'before/upon' and فرزند اوی = 'his son' (اوی is the older possessive/pronominal form). The subject is omitted (understood 'you').
segments.s521-g82: پرستنده‌ای is a substantive with the -ای ending giving an indefinite/nominal sense 'a worshipper/devotee'. The clause uses the noun before the pronoun تو (paratactic order common in Persian) to mean 'you are a worshipper'; the copula (هستی) is omitted. نه before پیوند negates 'kin/bond' (i.e. 'not kin'), and اوی again marks possession 'his'.
segments.s521-g83: نکردی is the past negative (2nd sg.) of کردن here meaning 'you did not make' or 'you would not have made'. به + تو functions as a directional/indirect-object phrase 'toward him/you' depending on context (here: 'to him'/'against him' is meant), and دشمنی is the noun 'enmity'. ار is the conditional 'if', introducing the protasis, and بدی is 'harm/evil' (the thing that would cause enmity).
segments.s521-g84: که is the relative/conjunction 'that/which' linking to بدی. خود 'yourself' emphasizes the actor. کرده‌ای is the perfect (present-past) 2nd-person form 'you have done' (کرده + ای). تو is the explicit pronoun for emphasis; به here means 'by/through' and links to the following noun نابخردی 'folly' (so به نابخردی = 'by/through folly').
segments.s521-g85: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. کام و خشنودی 'desire and approval/pleasure' are coordinated direct objects; او is the pronominal possessor 'his' attached after the nouns (classical order). بجوی is the 2nd-person singular imperative/ subjunctive of جستن 'seek' (command 'seek').
segments.s521-g86: مگردان is the negative imperative (do not turn/return) formed with the negative prefix مـ plus گردان (from گردیدن/گَردانیدن). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. فرمان او 'his command' is the object of the prohibition. هیچ روی is an idiomatic collocation meaning 'in no way'/'on no account' (literally 'no face' but used adverbially), so the whole phrase means 'do not by any means turn away from his order.'
segments.s521-g87: ز before a noun is a poetic preposition 'for/on account of' (here 'for a small sum of dinars'). دینار is the coin/ money; لختی here is an adverbial/quantifying word 'a little' or 'a small amount' modifying دینار. فرستادمت is past tense 'I sent you' (فرستادم + enclitic object مت), with the object clitic attached to the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s521-g88: به نامه درون is a locative/means phrase 'in/through a letter' (به with the instrumentive sense 'by/in' here). پندها is the plural noun 'advice(s)/admonitions' as the direct object. دادمت is 'I gave you' (دادم + enclitic مت), another past tense with the object clitic attached; the sequence means 'I gave you advice in a private/inner letter.'
segments.s521-g41: بدید is a third-person past tense verb ('he/one saw'). آن یکی is a noun phrase 'that one' (pointing to a particular person) with گور افگنده a past-participial adjective modifying it ('having made/dug a grave' or 'grave-cast'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' closing the clause. Word order is S V V (verb 'saw' then noun phrase then verb 'said'), a poetic arrangement where the participle modifies 'one'.
segments.s521-g42: که introduces the reported speech ('that/that which'). با دست آنکس is a prepositional phrase 'with/by that person's hand' (با + noun). هنر باد جفت is an optative construction: باد is the archaic optative particle 'may it be', هنر 'skill/art', and جفت 'paired/mate' — literally 'may skill be made pair', i.e. 'may his skill be matched with his hand'.
segments.s521-g43: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed, slightly archaic phrase 'thus gave answer' (چنین 'thus', داد past of 'give', پاسخ 'answer'). به + شاه اردشیر indicates the indirect object 'to King Ardashir' (بدو in later line is a contraction of به + او).
segments.s521-g44: که introduces reported content ('that'). این گور را marks 'this grave' as definite object with را. من is the emphatic subject 'I', and فگندم is first-person past 'I cast/struck/caused' (here 'made/caused the grave'). به تیر is an ablative/instrumental 'by/with an arrow' (به used to indicate instrument).
segments.s521-g45: پسر گفت is 'the son said' (subject + past verb). کین را is the definite object 'this vengeance' marked by را, من is the first-person pronoun 'I' for emphasis, and افگنده‌ام is the perfect/past-form with present relevance 'I have cast/inflicted' (compound past meaning 'I have done it').
segments.s521-g46: همان جفت را is 'that same pair/match' with را marking it as object/topic; نیز is 'also' linking this claim to the previous one; جوینده‌ام is an adjectival/nominal-present form 'I am a seeker' or 'I seek' (first-person present), so the clause means 'I also seek that same match.'
segments.s521-g47: چنین داد پاسخ repeats 'thus gave answer'; بدو is the contracted prepositional pronoun 'to him' (به + او), and اردشیر names the addressee. The phrase places the reply as directly given to Ardashir.
segments.s521-g48: که introduces the content of the reply ('that'). دشتی فراخست is a verbless/auxiliary construction meaning 'there is a wide plain' or 'the plain is wide' (دشتی 'plain' + فراخست 'was wide/expansive'); و هم گور و تیر lists additional items 'and also the grave and (the) arrow' with هم 'also' linking them. The clause enumerates what is present or notable in the scene.
segments.s521-g9: ابا here is an archaic spelling/placement for با (with) functioning as a preposition; the phrase ابا نامداران means “with the renowned (men).” بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' placed before its subject (poetic inversion is common). جوان is a noun 'the young man' and is the subject of بیامد.
segments.s521-g10: به جایی is a prepositional phrase 'to/into a place'; که introduces a relative clause modifying جایی. فرموده بود is an honorific/past construction meaning 'had said/ordered' (فرموده = said/commanded, بود as past auxiliary), and اردوان is the agent of that verb.
segments.s521-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as.' کرسی نهاد is a light past action 'placed/set the throne' (نهاد = past of نهادن). از بر is an archaic prepositional combination meaning 'upon/on', بر is 'on' and چرخ is 'the sky/heaven'; شید is a poetic epithet 'the beloved' or 'love‑mad' referring to the sitter and links back to the implied subject who placed the throne.
segments.s521-g12: جهان گشت is 'the world turned/became' with گشت as past of 'become/turn.' چون introduces a simile 'as/like'; روی here means 'face/complexion,' رومی qualifies that face as 'Roman,' and سپید describes it as 'white/pale.' Together: 'the world became like a Roman's pale face.'
segments.s521-g13: پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a worshipper/devotee' (object). پیش خواند is a compound idea 'called before/summoned' where خواند is used as 'summon/call,' and اردشیر is the agent placed after the verb (inverted word order typical in poetry): 'Ardashir summoned a devotee forward.'
segments.s521-g14: همان هدیه‌هایی means 'those same gifts.' که begins a relative clause 'which.' بد ناگزیر is an idiomatic phrase here meaning 'ill/under compulsion' — بد functions adverbially ('badly/reluctantly') modifying ناگزیر ('compelled/inevitable'), so the clause says these gifts were given under compulsion or unwillingly.
segments.s521-g15: فرستاد is a past tense verb 'sent/dispatched.' نزدیک شاه اردوان is a locative phrase 'near King Ardavan' (نزدیک = near). The agent who sends is implied earlier; here the phrase gives the destination/scene of the sending.
segments.s521-g16: فرستاده is a noun 'the envoy/messenger' (object of فرستاد) followed by the proper name بابک and the epithet پهلوان in apposition: 'the envoy, Babak the champion.' Persian often strings nouns/names without a copula; the whole phrase names who was sent.
segments.s521-g49: یکی دیگر (literally “one other”) functions here as “another.” افگن is an adjective meaning “shining/radiant.” بر + ین together form the poetic adjective برین “high/lofty” (the particle ین is an adjectival suffix here). هم = “also/too” and نشان = “sign/token”; the phrase is a nominal phrase “another shining, lofty sign.” Note the adjective-order typical of Persian poetry: determiner + adjective(s) + noun.
segments.s521-g50: دروغ is the fronted subject “falsehood/lie.” از here has the partitive/”one of/among” force (“one of the sins”), and گناهست is a fused poetic form of گناه است “is a sin” (predicate). بر سرکشان is a locative/target phrase meaning “upon/among the rebellious/proud ones”; the whole clause says that lying counts among their sins. Note the archaic/poetic fusion and inversion (subject fronted).
segments.s521-g51: پر از خشم شدن is an idiom meaning “to become full of anger.” پر = “full (of)”, از = linking preposition “of,” خشم = “anger,” and شد is the past-tense verb “became.” زان is a demonstrative “that” (hence “that young Ardavan”), followed by the name اردوان. Word order places the state-verb cluster before the explicit subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s521-g52: یکی is an indefinite subject “one/someone” (here effectively ‘he’). بانگ برزد is an archaic verbal phrase meaning “uttered/raised a cry” (برزد is an old-form verb used with بانگ). به marks the addressee/target “to/at,” and مرد جوان = “the young man.” The clause reports that (he) shouted to the young man.
segments.s521-g53: بدو گفت شاه is the inverted poetic word order for “the king said to him” (بدو = ‘to him,’ گفت here functions as the verb ‘said,’ and شاه is the subject ‘the king’ placed after the verb). این گناه منست is a copular clause: این گناه = “this fault/blame,” and منست is an archaic fused form expressing possession/assignment (here “is mine” / “belongs to me”).
segments.s521-g54: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause (“for/that”). پروردن is a verbal noun/infinitive “to raise/foster,” governing the objects آیین و راه (lit. “custom/rite and way/path”) — parallel nouns meaning the traditional order and the proper course. منست again states possession/responsibility (“is mine / is my duty”). The clause explains why the king claims the blame.
segments.s521-g55: ترا (here split as ت + را) is the accusative “you,” with خود for emphasis (“you yourself”). به بزم و به نخچیرگاه lists destinations with the preposition به: “to the banquet/feast and to the hunting-ground.” The phrase is the object‑part of the following rhetorical question.
segments.s521-g56: چرا begins the rhetorical question “why?” برد here is an archaic form of the verb بردن “to take” (so چرا برد = “why should (I) take”), followed by باید “must/ought” which states necessity. همی is an emphatic particle (“indeed/surely”), and با سپاه = “with the army.” The clause means that instead of being led to feasts and hunting, he ought to go with the army.
segments.s521-g65: This is a short SOV-style clause in classical Persian: 'یکی' (an indefinite subject: 'a certain one/someone') precedes the object 'نامه' and the past verb 'بنوشت' (3rd singular past, archaic/poetic form of نوشت). 'پیش نیا' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/to Nia' (پیش + noun) that indicates the addressee/location. The verb بنوشت is the main past tense action 'wrote'.
segments.s521-g66: This is a pair of parallel adjectival phrases using the pattern 'پر از X' = 'full of X', but rendered poetically with the noun before the adjective ('پر از غم دل' = 'heart full of sorrow'). The coordinating 'و' links the two halves; the second half repeats 'پر از' for emphasis ('سر پر از کیمیا' = 'head/mind full of the elixir/secret'). Note the inverted word order (noun before the 'full of' predicate) typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s521-g67: The clause begins with 'که' introducing the content ('what') and contains an indirect interrogative: 'ما را' (object/experiencer marked by the postposition را) + 'چه پیش آمد' (the idiom 'چه پیش آمد' = 'what happened/befell'). 'از اردوان' indicates the agent/source ('from Ardavan'). The verb آمد here carries the sense 'happened/befell'.
segments.s521-g68: This clause gives a comment or exclamation introduced again by 'که'. 'درد تنش' = 'the pain of his body' (noun + possessive enclitic ش), and 'رنج روان' = 'the suffering of the soul'. The word 'باد' here is the optative/particle meaning 'may it be' (an older/poetic wish form), so the whole phrase expresses a wish or wonder that his body be in pain and his soul in torment.
segments.s521-g69: 'همه یاد کرد' is a common Persian semi-verb phrase meaning 'he recalled/remembered everything' (یاد 'remembrance' + کرد 'did' = 'remembered'). The following subordinate clause 'آن کجا رفته بود' (that where he had gone) uses past perfect 'رفته بود' (رفته + auxiliary بود) to indicate the prior action.
segments.s521-g70: This is an echoing question/relative clause listing details: 'کجا اردوان' = 'where Ardavan (was/gone)' and 'از چه آشفته بود' = 'for what reason he was upset'. Here 'از' takes the sense 'because of' and 'آشفته بود' is past tense 'was agitated/distraught'. The word order is emphatic/poetic (question words fronted).
segments.s521-g71: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause 'آن نامه نزدیک بابک رسید' places the demonstrative + object before the verb in the typical SOV pattern: 'that letter reached near Babak' (نزدیک + proper name indicates approach/arrival at Babak). 'رسید' is simple past 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s521-g72: This negative clause uses the prefixed negative + past verb 'نکرد' ('did not do') plus an infinitival/participial sense with 'پدید' ('make/let appear'). Literally 'he did not make that speech appear to anyone.' 'نیز' here functions as 'either/also' in the negative context, and 'بر کس' means 'to/for any person'. The construction is idiomatic: 'نکرد ... پدید' = 'did not disclose/reveal'.

sentences.s521-s1: When he came near the palace, they told the king about that petitioner.
sentences.s521-s2: He summoned the young man before Ardavan with favour, and uttered many words about Babak.
sentences.s521-s3: He seated him near the throne, and in the courtyard he made a place for him.
sentences.s521-s4: He sent every kind of food, and likewise clothing and things to be spread out.
sentences.s521-s49: He chose a spot near the horses' stable; he did not take a place among the dining/feeding area.
sentences.s521-s50: Day and night his business was eating and evil deeds; wine and the cup and the entertainers were his companions.
sentences.s521-s17: All the swift-footed sprang up; meanwhile the dust mingled with its fellows.
sentences.s521-s18: He galloped forward straight toward Ardashir's inner court; when he drew near he fitted an arrow to his bow.
sentences.s521-s19: He struck at the cypresses one male onager; an arrow and a feather passed over the onager.
sentences.s521-s20: At that same time Ardavan also came; he saw that it was open and (he saw) the young man upon it.
sentences.s521-s37: His heart became full of pain and sorrow because of that affair; he brought a few dinars from the treasure.
sentences.s521-s38: He sent to him ten thousand (men/units); they raised dust and horsemen.
sentences.s521-s39: He ordered that a scribe be brought before him; a certain letter was composed for Ardashir.
sentences.s521-s40: That this inexperienced, little-witted young man, when he went hunting, went with Ardavan.
sentences.s521-s9: Ardavan saw him and took a liking to him; he found the young man useful to him.
sentences.s521-s10: The king kept him like a son; he never left him without care even for a time.
sentences.s521-s11: He kept him at meals, at the table and in the hunting-ground, in his own presence for years and months.
sentences.s521-s12: He treated him like his own child and did not separate him from his own ties.
sentences.s521-s29: Know this: that you will surpass my son; choose loftiness and Kandauri.
sentences.s521-s30: Go; look at our Tazi horses, and also choose that spot to settle/build a dwelling.
sentences.s521-s31: Be the horses' ultimate commander; in every affair be a companion to every person.
sentences.s521-s32: Ardashir came, his eyes full of water (tears); he was forced onto the rear of the horse.
sentences.s521-s45: Whenever you put this sum to use, choose another course until time has passed.
sentences.s521-s46: The seasoned, world‑wise old rider came galloping up to Ardashir.
sentences.s521-s47: When he read that letter aloud he became pleased; his heart turned toward stratagem and Arvand.
sentences.s521-s48: He spread out every kind of spreadable item, from garments to food.
sentences.s521-s13: So it was that one day at the hunting-ground the army and the king's sons were scattered.
sentences.s521-s14: The unpleasant (ill-pleasing) king kept driving Ardashir the brave along with Ardavan.
sentences.s521-s15: King Ardavan had four sons; each of those, one like another, was a ruler.
sentences.s521-s16: On the plain there appeared from afar a gūr (wild ass); from them the hungry troops rose up in tumult.
sentences.s521-s41: Why did you rush upon his son? You are his worshipper, not his kin.
sentences.s521-s42: You would not have made him your enemy if you had not done harm — harm which you yourself have done out of folly.
sentences.s521-s43: Now seek his desire and approval; do not turn away from his command in any way.
sentences.s521-s44: For a little dinar I sent you away; I gave you counsel inside a letter.
sentences.s521-s21: One of them, seeing the grave he had made, said: "May that man's skill be paired with his hand."
sentences.s521-s22: Thus he answered King Ardashir: "I made this grave with an arrow."
sentences.s521-s23: The son said: "I have inflicted this vengeance; I too am seeking that same match."
sentences.s521-s24: Thus he answered Ardashir to him: "There is a wide plain — and also a grave and an arrow."
sentences.s521-s5: The young man came with the renowned ones to the place that Ardavan had ordered.
sentences.s521-s6: When he set the throne upon the vault of heaven, the world became like the pale face of a Roman.
sentences.s521-s7: Ardashir summoned a devotee before him — those very gifts which he had been compelled (to give).
sentences.s521-s8: He sent Babak the champion as envoy near King Ardavan.
sentences.s521-s25: Another shining, high token — falsehood is among the sins of the rebellious.
sentences.s521-s26: That young Ardavan was filled with anger; he cried out to the young man.
sentences.s521-s27: The king said to him, “This blame is mine; for nurturing our custom and way is my responsibility.”
sentences.s521-s28: Why should I take you to feasts and to the hunting-ground? You must indeed go with the army.
sentences.s521-s33: A certain man wrote a letter to Nia, his heart full of sorrow and his head full of the elixir.
sentences.s521-s34: What has befallen us from Ardavan, that his body should be in pain and his soul in anguish?
sentences.s521-s35: He called everything to mind — where that one had gone, where Ardavan was, and why he was distraught.
sentences.s521-s36: When that letter reached Babak, he did not reveal those words to anyone either.
